text: Test soil pH and add lime if needed
notes: April timing - lime application can be done now or saved for winter
subtasks: Purchase lime if pH is low

text: Overseed thin and bare patches in lawn
notes: April-May timing for spring overseeding

text: Maintain proper mowing height at 3-3.5 inches
notes: All season maintenance - ongoing through fall
subtasks: Never cut more than 1/3 of grass height

text: Begin deep watering schedule (1 inch/week)
notes: Summer watering plan - starts June, no fertilizing during summer

text: Overseed entire lawn (fall priority #1)
notes: Early September - most important annual task

text: Apply fall fertilizer (September application)
notes: Early September fertilization - priority #2

text: Apply late fall fertilizer (October/November application)
notes: Late October/Early November - priority #2

Giant: white bread and fresh mint
Buy garden bed supplies and lay cardboard
notes: Day 1 of garden bed project in backyard near walkout
subtasks: Lay cardboard on 4x8 area with 6-inch overlaps

text: Mix amendments and layer on garden bed
notes: Days 2-3 of garden bed project. Blood meal needs to mellow to avoid burning roots
subtasks: Mix blood meal and bone meal into compost/topsoil
Layer 4-6 inches of mixture on wet cardboard

text: Buy transplants and set up garden supports
notes: Week 2 of garden project. Look for stocky, short transplants not tall leggy ones
subtasks: Buy stocky tomato transplants (avoid leggy ones)
Get soil thermometer to check temperature

text: Check soil temperature for planting readiness
notes: Don't plant until soil consistently reads 65°F. This is days 10-14 of project
subtasks: Use thermometer to check soil temp
Verify soil has been undisturbed for 5-7 days minimum

text: Plant garden bed crops
notes: Planting day (day 10-14). Tomatoes root along buried stem. Handle cucumber roots gently. Peppers like heat and benefit from tomato shade later
subtasks: Plant cucumbers on north end with trellis
Plant 2 tomatoes in center with cages (bury deep to first leaves)
Plant 2-3 peppers on south end (same depth as pot)
Water in with diluted fish emulsion immediately

text: Establish watering routine and mulch plants
notes: Weeks 3-4. These crops hate wet/dry cycles. Let plants establish before fertilizing
subtasks: Water consistently 1 inch per week minimum
Mulch with straw or wood chips 3-5 days after planting

text: Side-dress plants when flowering begins
notes: Week 6 when flowers appear. Avoid high nitrogen which creates huge plants with little fruit
subtasks: Apply blood meal lightly around each plant
Switch to low-nitrogen, higher phosphorus fertilizer (like 5-10-5)
Focus on fruit set rather than leaf growth

text: Ongoing summer garden maintenance
notes: Ongoing through summer. Don't leave overripe cucumbers on vine as it stops production
subtasks: Pinch tomato suckers weekly, keep 1-2 main stems
Harvest cucumbers aggressively to keep plants producing
Leave peppers largely alone (they don't like fussing)

text: Consider having JP help with the physical aspects of garden bed construction as an educational project
notes: From counsel suggestion

text: Establish watering routine and mulch plants
notes: Weeks 3-4. Don't fertilize again yet during establishment period.
subtasks: Water consistently 1 inch per week minimum
Mulch around plants with straw or wood chips
Wait 3-5 days after planting before mulching

text: Switch to flowering fertilizer when flowers appear
notes: Week 6. Most gardeners make mistake of continuing nitrogen feeding.
subtasks: Side-dress lightly with blood meal around each plant
Switch to low-nitrogen, higher phosphorus liquid fertilizer (5-10-5 ratio)
Focus on fruit set rather than leaf growth

text: Begin ongoing summer garden maintenance
notes: Ongoing through summer. Year one is experimental - bed will be better in year two.
subtasks: Pinch tomato suckers weekly, keep to 1-2 main stems
Harvest cucumbers aggressively to keep production going
Leave peppers alone - they don't like fussing
Monitor for drainage issues and weed pressure from edges

text: The soil temperature check task might need to be repeated daily until conditions are right - consider setting up a recurring reminder
notes: From counsel suggestion

text: Consider involving JP or Joseph in some of the garden tasks as educational opportunities, especially the ongoing maintenance tasks
notes: From counsel suggestion

text: Create safe storage plan for fish emulsion and fertilizers away from Michael
notes: Michael is at age where anything in a bottle looks interesting - needs secure storage location

text: Discuss produce preservation preferences with Lorenzo
notes: Clarify how to handle expected tomato yield (10-20 lbs per plant) and cucumber preservation methods before mid-July harvest

text: Plan sun protection and hydration for garden work days
notes: Late April through summer means real heat exposure during soil prep and planting - ensure supplies for anyone doing outdoor work

text: Arrange childcare coverage for James during heavy garden work
notes: James (13 months) cannot be underfoot during digging, cardboard laying, and soil amendment handling - needs containment or another caregiver for April 28th and 30th prep work

text: Arrange JP or Joseph to help with compost hauling and trellis setup
notes: Functional movement activity that teaches useful skills - can supervise Michael's participation as well

text: Research current hours and activities at Snead's Farm
notes: Prepare for potential family visits in spring/fall

text: Check for U-pick or farm stand options at the asparagus farm
notes: Asparagus may be in season mid-May; consider bringing a cooler for fresh produce

text: Verify picnic area availability and outside food policy
notes: Important for planning lunch with four kids including a toddler; ensure options for James's dietary needs
